picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA remains the apex AI infrastructure compounder — hyperscalers 'don't have many options' per a 5-star analyst reaffirming a $350 price target as of June 5, with the Blackwell ramp sustaining outsized data center revenue growth. The June 5 selloff triggered by AVGO's guidance miss was contagion, not fundamental deterioration, creating a tactical re-entry point. Momentum screen ranks it #2 in the universe with consistent EPS beats across all 4 quarters; Jensen Huang's ecosystem dominance (NVLink, CUDA) constitutes an unmatched switching-cost moat that justifies a premium multiple in the AI supercycle.
picks.catalyst: Next earnings report (Q2 FY2027, expected late August) with Blackwell NVL72 shipment ramp confirmation and any hyperscaler capex guidance update
picks.entryNote: Pulled back ~3-5% from recent highs on the AVGO-led AI chip selloff June 5; trading at ~35x forward earnings vs. 5-year avg of ~28x but justified by 100%+ data center YoY growth; consolidating near 50-day moving average — constructive technical setup post-flush
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of hyperscaler design wins to AMD or custom silicon (Google TPU/AWS Trainium) causing data center revenue growth to decelerate below 40% YoY, or gross margin compression below 70%
picks.keyRisk: Hyperscaler capex digestion pause or customer shift to custom ASICs reducing GPU demand faster than consensus models

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL delivered its strongest cloud print in years — 63% Google Cloud growth to $20B with operating margin expanding to 36.1% and EPS up 82% YoY in Q1 2026, with cloud backlog nearly doubling QoQ to $460B. A newly announced $920M/month SpaceX cloud deal signed June 5 further validates Google Cloud's enterprise momentum. Berkshire Hathaway nearly tripled its GOOGL stake in Q1 2026 (buying ~40M additional shares to ~57.8M total), while GOOGL trades at a reasonable ~20x forward earnings vs. mega-cap tech peers — the best quality/momentum combination at relative value.
picks.catalyst: May CPI print on June 10 (macro event) and Q2 2026 earnings in late July with Google Cloud trajectory and AI search monetization update
picks.entryNote: Pulled back modestly from post-Q1 surge (+10% day); ~20x forward P/E is a discount to historical averages and well below MSFT/META peers; SpaceX deal announced June 5 at $920M/month is a clear revenue visibility signal not yet fully priced in
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 40% YoY, or DOJ antitrust remedies impose structural separation of Google Search from Chrome/Android distribution network
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust structural remedies in the ongoing search case could force a breakup of search distribution channels, permanently compressing search revenue growth

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO was cut from the #1 slot last week after its post-earnings -14% drop, but the thesis is unbroken — the business delivered its 6th consecutive double beat with 48% YoY revenue growth and 143% AI semiconductor growth, and management guided Q3 to $29.4B (+84% YoY) with a line-of-sight to $100B+ in annual custom AI chip revenue by end-2027. The selloff was an expectations reset on a modest AI guide miss vs. the most aggressive buyside estimates, not a fundamental deterioration. At ~$395 (vs. the $495 all-time high on June 3) with 48 analysts rating it Strong Buy and a consensus PT of $520, the asymmetry is clearly to the upside. Management projects multi-gigawatt custom chip deployments for Google, Anthropic, Meta, and others under contracts extending to 2031.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings on September 3, 2026 — first major print to validate the $29.4B revenue guide and confirm AI segment expansion beyond 143% YoY
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$395, down ~20% from its June 3 all-time high of $495; 52-week range $241–$495; 48-analyst consensus PT of $520 implies 31% upside from current; post-earnings dip with strong analyst support (upgraded to Buy at Erste Group June 5) is a historically favorable re-entry setup for quality compounders
picks.exitTrigger: Q3 revenue guide below $27B, loss of hyperscaler custom ASIC contracts, or VMware infrastructure software ARR declining meaningfully suggesting churn post-acquisition
picks.keyRisk: AI guide miss in September (Q3 earnings) re-igniting concerns that $100B 2027 target is aspirational rather than contracted, causing a further multiple derating

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon is the top conviction add for Tepper (Appaloosa doubled the stake to ~15.2% of portfolio) and Ackman (Pershing moved AMZN to #2 holding), both citing AWS AI infrastructure capex converting into margin expansion. AWS remains the dominant cloud provider with Trainium/Inferentia custom silicon reducing reliance on NVDA while expanding cloud margins — a unique structural hedge within the AI ecosystem. At ~15-17x forward earnings backed by e-commerce profitability and an AWS operating margin trajectory above 40%, AMZN is the best risk-adjusted large-cap AI infrastructure play at current valuations.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July/early August) with AWS revenue growth trajectory and operating margin expansion confirmation; Project Kuiper satellite internet launch update
picks.entryNote: Pulled back with the broader tech selloff on June 5; remains ~15-17x forward earnings — the cheapest mega-cap tech on forward P/E; Tepper's 98% position increase and Ackman's move to #2 holding provide strong institutional validation at current levels
picks.exitTrigger: AWS revenue growth decelerates below 20% YoY, or capex investment cycle shows negative ROI with operating margins compressing below 8% at the company level
picks.keyRisk: Overinvestment in AI capex (Project Kuiper, AWS expansion) suppressing FCF for longer than consensus expects, while AWS faces intensifying competition from Azure and Google Cloud

picks.name: Oracle Corporation
picks.rationale: Oracle is a high-conviction carry-over from last week with an upcoming catalyst — MU vs. ORCL debate is active ahead of ORCL Q4 FY2026 earnings. Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) has been taking AI workloads from hyperscalers, with OCI GPU clusters in high demand from frontier AI labs seeking lower-cost training environments. Druckenmiller added new tech positions in this cycle; ORCL's expanding AI infrastructure contracts and multi-cloud database strategy (Autonomous Database + OCI) have driven consistent double-digit revenue acceleration, with analysts expecting 15-20% cloud revenue growth for FY2027.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings (expected mid-June 2026) — cloud infrastructure revenue growth rate and remaining performance obligations guidance
picks.entryNote: Consolidating near 52-week highs after strong momentum; OCI GPU cluster demand thesis reinforced by AI lab expansion; trading at ~25-28x forward earnings, a slight premium to 5-year average but justified by accelerating cloud mix shift and expanding operating leverage
picks.exitTrigger: OCI cloud revenue growth decelerates below 30% YoY, or hyperscaler competition (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud) begins materially displacing ORCL's database cloud migration wins
picks.keyRisk: ORCL's cloud transition still depends heavily on legacy database renewal contracts; any slowdown in the enterprise database-to-cloud migration pace would compress growth assumptions significantly

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: META carries over from last week with the thesis fully intact — Q1 2026 delivered a 57% EPS beat ($10.44 actual vs. consensus), 33% YoY revenue growth, and Advantage+ AI ad monetization continues driving CPM expansion. Despite being Coatue's largest U.S. holding even after a 23% trim, META's forward P/E remains in the mid-20s with gross margins near 80% and FCF yield among the highest in mega-cap tech. The inflation/stagflationary regime actually benefits META as nominal GDP growth keeps ad budgets resilient even as real wages fall — advertisers spend to protect market share.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with Llama 4 AI model monetization update and Advantage+ ad revenue contribution disclosure
picks.entryNote: Mid-20s forward P/E is a discount to peers given 33% revenue growth; pulled back with tech on June 5; Cathie Wood noted loading up on META pullbacks June 5; price/fundamental convergence is highly favorable with FCF growing faster than revenue
picks.exitTrigger: Digital advertising revenue growth decelerates below 15% YoY, or major regulatory breakup (Instagram/WhatsApp divestiture) materially disrupts the integrated ad targeting model
picks.keyRisk: EU Digital Markets Act enforcement or US antitrust remedy forcing divestiture of Instagram or WhatsApp, permanently fragmenting Meta's cross-platform advertising data advantage

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: GS is a core carry-over from last week — the steepening yield curve (2s10s at +38 bps, 30-year at 5.01%) directly expands NIM and capital markets activity. Goldman Sachs itself raised the S&P 500 year-end target to 8,000 with $340 EPS forecast, reflecting internal confidence in the M&A/IPO revival cycle. Capital markets income, trading revenue, and private credit advisory are all structurally growing; XLF receives an Outperform rating from Schwab, and financials are attracting rotation from tech amid the post-payrolls re-pricing. The SpaceX IPO pipeline and AI-sector M&A wave are direct GS revenue catalysts.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) — investment banking fee acceleration, trading revenue from June 5 volatility spike, and SpaceX IPO advisory mandate confirmation
picks.entryNote: XLF trades at ~14-15x forward P/E, a significant discount to the broader index; GS specifically benefits from steeper curve and elevated capital markets activity; pulled back with the June 5 selloff providing a modestly improved entry; rising rate-hike odds are a moderate headwind but capital markets revenue more than offsets NIM sensitivity
picks.exitTrigger: Investment banking deal flow collapses (M&A backlog falls >30%), credit spreads widen materially above 150 bps HY (signal of credit stress), or a major proprietary trading loss exceeding $2B in a single quarter
picks.keyRisk: A hard landing scenario triggering credit cycle turn and collapsing M&A/IPO pipeline simultaneously — GS is highly correlated to capital markets sentiment

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is a core carry-over with the thesis fully intact — the Iran War and geopolitical escalation have driven defense budgets higher across NATO and the Middle East, creating a durable multi-year revenue backlog for Pratt & Whitney engines and Raytheon missile/defense systems. Industrials sector had a 24.19% total return over the past year, with the trifecta of defense spending, AI power infrastructure, and reshoring manufacturing creating durable multi-year earnings support. RTX's commercial aerospace recovery (Pratt engines powering the aircraft rebound) adds a second secular growth leg independent of geopolitics.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Pratt & Whitney GTF engine delivery ramp update and Pentagon contract awards including any new hypersonic/directed energy wins
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~19-21x forward earnings, at or near 5-year average; relatively low beta (~0.9) vs. pure tech, making it a quality industrial compounder with defensive characteristics; Iran war geopolitical risk premium not fully reflected in consensus defense budget assumptions
picks.exitTrigger: Iran ceasefire or major diplomatic resolution materially reducing geopolitical risk premium; Pratt & Whitney GTF powder metal engine recall expanding beyond current scope and costing >$10B in additional remediation
picks.keyRisk: GTF powder metal engine recall scope expansion — if more engines require removal than currently disclosed, the financial liability and delivery schedule disruption would compress margins and free cash flow significantly

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM is a core carry-over and remains the highest-conviction energy play — Energy is the only sector trading at a discount both to the index AND to its own 10-year average (~32% on each measure), with the broadest positive earnings revision breadth in the S&P 500. The Strait of Hormuz closure from the Iran war has structurally elevated oil prices; Bloomberg Commodity Index up +40.5% YoY confirms the commodity supercycle impulse. XOM's Pioneer acquisition has expanded low-cost Permian Basin production capacity, providing superior FCF at $70+ oil while maintaining dividend growth. Morgan Stanley's base case of ~$90/barrel by year-end provides further upside.
picks.catalyst: May CPI print June 10 (elevated energy component) and Q2 2026 earnings (late July) reflecting full-quarter impact of elevated oil/gas prices from Iran war disruption
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~12-14x forward earnings, a 32% discount to the S&P 500 multiple and to XOM's own 10-year average; energy sector has the widest positive earnings revision breadth; Chevron was reduced by Berkshire (-35%) while XOM held, a subtle signal of relative quality preference within energy
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained crude oil decline below $65/barrel (Hormuz resolution + Saudi supply increase), or a U.S. windfall profits tax targeting energy companies exceeding a defined ROE threshold
picks.keyRisk: Rapid Iran ceasefire and Hormuz reopening combined with a coordinated OPEC+ production increase collapsing the oil price support that underpins the entire energy thesis

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a steady carry-over — as a dominant ratings, data, and analytics business, it benefits directly from the surge in corporate debt issuance (IG OAS ~80 bps supports active issuance), AI-driven data product demand, and the capital markets revival cycle. SPGI's ratings segment generates high-margin, recurring fee income from every bond issued, making it a financial infrastructure compounder that benefits whether rates are rising or falling as long as issuance volumes remain elevated. The AI capex supercycle requires massive debt financing — every hyperscaler bond issuance is a SPGI revenue event.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — debt issuance volume data and Market Intelligence AI product revenue traction disclosure
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~28-30x forward earnings, a modest premium to 5-year average but justified by secular data/analytics growth; pulled back modestly with the June 5 rate-hike fear spike; rate volatility actually increases demand for SPGI's risk analytics and ratings services
picks.exitTrigger: Debt issuance volumes collapse below 2020 levels (credit freeze), or a major regulatory restructuring of the ratings duopoly that introduces pricing pressure or mandated competition
picks.keyRisk: A credit market freeze driven by a hard landing or sovereign debt panic reduces bond issuance volumes by 30%+ and directly compresses ratings segment revenue

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: GLD is the highest-conviction defensive position in a stagflationary, late-cycle regime. Goldman Sachs expects continued strength as central bank reallocations persist; rising macro risks from tariffs, high deficits, and current account deterioration have structurally damaged dollar attractiveness. With headline CPI at 3.8% and re-accelerating, the Iran war keeping the energy component elevated, and the Moody's AAA downgrade already in the rearview, gold's structural bid from USD debasement and fiscal risk is as strong as it has been since 2020. GLD provides zero-correlation protection against corporate earnings risk and the key left-tail events (stagflation lock-in, sovereign debt spiral).
picks.catalyst: May CPI print June 10 — a hot print above 4.2% would push rate-hike odds sharply higher, strengthening gold's real-rate and fiscal-risk bid simultaneously
picks.entryNote: Structurally elevated on the commodity supercycle; Bloomberg Commodity Index up 40.5% YoY; gold remains supported at current levels with Goldman's structural bull thesis intact; not at a technical extreme — constructive for new capital
picks.exitTrigger: Fed achieves credible 2% inflation trajectory with rate cuts materializing, USD strengthens materially (+5%+ DXY), and geopolitical tensions resolve (Iran ceasefire + Hormuz reopening)
picks.keyRisk: A Fed hawkish surprise that raises real rates significantly above +200 bps (nominal ~6%+) could trigger gold selling as the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding bullion rises materially

picks.name: Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides quality-screened dividend payers (FCF yield, dividend growth, low debt-to-equity) with a ~3.25% yield — approximately 3x the S&P 500 dividend yield — that exhibited significantly lower drawdowns during the April 2025 Liberation Day selloff vs. high-multiple growth names. In a late-cycle, stagflationary regime with negative real wages and rising rate-hike odds, dividend quality factors with financials and industrials concentration (~20% each) are both income generators and relative safe havens. SCHD's $85B scale provides liquidity and its quality screens naturally exclude the most rate-sensitive, over-leveraged businesses.
picks.catalyst: May CPI print June 10 — if inflation surprises to the upside, rotation into dividend quality accelerates as growth multiples compress; monthly dividend distributions provide ongoing income regardless
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; key rotation trigger is June 10 CPI
picks.entryNote: 52-week range $26.16–$32.91; currently recovering near the top of range post-April volatility; dividend yield ~3.25% is attractive relative to 10yr Treasury at 4.55% on a risk-adjusted basis given equity upside optionality; factor tilt means low correlation to mega-cap tech concentration risk
picks.exitTrigger: Fed begins sustained rate-cut cycle (>150 bps in 12 months), making growth factor outperformance likely and reducing the relative appeal of dividend income; or credit cycle turn causing financials concentration to generate losses
picks.keyRisk: Financials concentration (~20%) means SCHD is exposed to a credit cycle turn — if bank NIM deteriorates sharply or credit losses emerge, the defensive characteristics are compromised

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Class B
picks.rationale: BRK.B is the highest-quality defensive equity — $397.4B in cash (up 6.5% from year-end), a $235M buyback restart under Greg Abel, and a DCF-implied 36.4% discount to fair value. The float-funded insurance model (GEICO, Gen Re) thrives in the hard insurance market while BNSF and BHE provide inflation-linked cash flows. Historically fell only ~3% in the 2022 drawdown vs. S&P -19%, making it the purest large-cap equity 'shock absorber' in the universe. Abel's aggressive Q1 restructuring — tripling GOOGL, exiting Visa/MC/Amazon/UNH — shows decisive capital allocation that markets are still digesting.
picks.catalyst: Any market correction >10% would activate Berkshire's $397B war chest for opportunistic acquisitions or repurchases — Abel has explicitly signaled deployment readiness; next 13F filing (August 15) will reveal Q2 capital allocation decisions
picks.entryNote: DCF model implies ~36% discount to intrinsic value at ~$499; P/B near 1.5x vs. historical average of 1.4x — essentially at fair value on book but below intrinsic; beta ~0.85 provides natural portfolio dampening; post-Buffett era transition is priced in, creating relative undervaluation vs. quality peers
picks.exitTrigger: Greg Abel makes a large, value-destroying acquisition (>$50B at a significant premium) that misallocates the cash war chest; or GEICO sustained underwriting losses causing insurance float to deteriorate materially
picks.keyRisk: Post-Buffett capital allocation uncertainty — Abel's track record is less established than Buffett's, and a major acquisition misstep with the $397B war chest would be a permanent capital impairment event

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury ETF
picks.rationale: IEF serves as the portfolio's primary left-tail recession hedge — government bonds are the most effective hedge against the largest equity risks (recession or AI optimism reversal), particularly at the 7-10 year duration sweet spot. If the stagflation scenario tips into demand destruction (real wages negative, savings rate at half pre-2022 levels), the Fed eventually cuts, and IEF rallies sharply. At a 10-year yield of 4.55%, IEF offers the highest yield entry in years as a tail hedge. Sized at 4% of book — meaningful enough to provide portfolio cushion, small enough not to drag on equity upside.
picks.catalyst: Any upside surprise in unemployment (above 4.7%) or a recession signal from ISM Manufacturing below 45 would trigger a flight-to-quality rally in intermediate Treasuries
picks.catalystWindow: June 10 CPI and ongoing macro data; BOJ June 16 decision (yen carry unwind = Treasury safety bid)
picks.entryNote: 10-year yield at 4.55% — highest in several years — makes IEF the best entry point since 2023 for a tail hedge; duration ~7.5 years provides meaningful price appreciation in a risk-off scenario without the extreme fiscal risk of TLT's 20+ year duration; BOJ June 16 hike risk could trigger a yen carry unwind benefiting Treasuries
picks.exitTrigger: 30-year Treasury yield breaks above 5.75% (fiscal deterioration / bond vigilante scenario) signaling IEF is no longer a safe hedge; or inflation remains above 4% for 6+ consecutive months with no recession signal, eliminating the Fed-cut optionality
picks.keyRisk: Fiscal deterioration and 'bond vigilante' re-emergence causing the correlation between equities and Treasuries to remain positive (both fall simultaneously) — the stagflation scenario where IEF provides no hedge benefit

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO rounds out the defensive sleeve as the premier consumer staples compounder — Berkshire Hathaway's 3rd largest holding at 11.56% of the portfolio, providing validation from the world's most disciplined capital allocator. In a stagflationary regime with negative real wages, KO's brand pricing power, global EM exposure (inflation hedge via local-currency revenue), and ~3% dividend yield provide income and ballast. KO's correlation to the high-multiple tech names driving portfolio volatility is near-zero, and its regulated/consumer-staple cash flows act as natural portfolio shock absorbers when AI sentiment turns.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — volume recovery in EM markets (Latin America, Africa) and pricing realization vs. inflation cost pass-through update
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~20x forward earnings, slightly above 10-year median (~18x) — a modest premium for the highest-quality consumer defensive compounder; dividend yield ~3.0% provides income floor; Berkshire's continued 11.56% allocation despite portfolio restructuring signals conviction in the thesis
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained volume decline (>3% YoY) driven by GLP-1 drug adoption reducing sugary beverage consumption, combined with inability to offset with price increases and EM volume growth
picks.keyRisk: GLP-1 obesity drug adoption (LLY's Mounjaro, NVO's Ozempic) meaningfully reducing sugary beverage consumption faster than consensus models, permanently impairing KO's volume growth trajectory

summary: The portfolio expresses a Cautiously Bullish, late-cycle stagflationary regime view: the AI capex supercycle remains the single most powerful earnings revision engine in the market (with Goldman projecting $800B in hyperscaler capex this year), but the dual Iran war energy shock and hot jobs print (172K vs. 85K est.) are pushing rate-hike odds to 57% and compressing multiples for the most speculative names. The growth book is anchored by NVDA, GOOGL, AVGO, and AMZN as the core AI infrastructure layer with differentiated value-chain exposure, balanced by META/ORCL for AI monetization and software infrastructure, GS/SPGI for the capital markets revival benefiting from the debt-issuance surge, and RTX/XOM as the geopolitical inflation beneficiaries that are uncorrelated to tech sentiment. The AVGO post-earnings pullback (~20% from ATH) is treated as an expectations reset rather than a thesis break, with the buy-the-dip thesis supported by 48 analysts maintaining Strong Buy and a $520 consensus PT vs. ~$395 current price. The dominant risk being underwritten is a stagflation lock-in driven by May CPI printing above 4.2% on June 10, which would force the Fed's hand and compress multiples for the growth book — the defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) is specifically positioned to hedge this scenario with gold as the primary stagflation hedge, IEF as the recession tail hedge, and BRK.B/SCHD/KO as low-beta quality compounders.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans 5 GICS sectors: Information Technology (NVDA, AVGO, ORCL — 3 names, at hard constraint limit but differentiated by GPU/custom silicon/cloud infra value chains), Communication Services (GOOGL, META), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN — AWS/cloud moat justifies placement), Financials (GS, SPGI), Industrials (RTX), and Energy (XOM); concentration in AI infrastructure is acknowledged (NVDA, AVGO, GOOGL, AMZN all benefit from the AI capex cycle) but each occupies a different layer — GPU compute, custom ASIC/networking, AI search/cloud, and cloud/logistics/satellite — providing meaningful differentiation. The defensive sleeve spans 4 distinct exposures: gold/commodity hedge (GLD), broad dividend/quality income (SCHD), equity insurance/conglomerate (BRK.B), intermediate long-duration Treasuries (IEF), and consumer staples (KO), with no duplicate exposure categories.

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet has been the highest-conviction carry-over since May 27, and today's selloff — driven by semi-specific AVGO miss and hawkish NFP — does not impair the GOOGL thesis. Google Cloud grew +63% in Q1 2026 following +48% in Q4 2025, Search accelerated +19% YoY, and operating margins expanded as AI workloads scaled. Berkshire added 225% to its GOOGL stake in Q1 2026, making it their 5th largest position. Today's broad tape selloff is an entry improvement, not a thesis break.
picks.catalyst: June 10 May CPI print + Q2 earnings (late July) with Cloud and Search guidance
picks.entryNote: Trading at reasonable forward multiple vs. growth rate; Cloud reacceleration justifies premium. Today's tech-wide selloff improves risk/reward at current prices vs. 5-day highs.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 40% YoY, or antitrust ruling mandating Search divestiture
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust forced restructuring of Search distribution agreements; AI Overviews cannibalizes ad clicks at scale

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA remains the dominant AI infrastructure pick despite today's semi selloff (-4%+ intraday). The AVGO miss was company-specific to custom ASIC guidance — NVDA's Blackwell/Rubin roadmap is intact, with Jensen Huang indicating up to $1 trillion in purchase orders through 2027. Q1 FY2027 revenue of $81.6B beat by $2.7B with net income at $58.3B vs. $42.9B expected. KeyBanc reaffirmed NVDA as 'best AI chip play' in the sector on today's tape. Today's dip is an accumulation opportunity, not a thesis break.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings (August) with Blackwell ramp confirmation and export-control-adjusted guidance
picks.entryNote: Down ~4% today from recent highs; still richly valued at ~35x forward earnings but supported by 100%+ YoY EPS growth. Today's dip toward 50dma creates better risk/reward than last week's highs.
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin compression below 68%, or loss of a major hyperscaler design win to AMD or custom silicon
picks.keyRisk: Export controls on H20/Blackwell further restricting China revenue, which still represents ~15% of total

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon is the cleanest multi-engine compounder in the universe: AWS reaccelerated to +28% YoY in Q1 (vs. +24% Q4 2025), Bedrock processed more tokens in Q1 2026 than all prior years combined, and Trainium custom silicon now runs at $20B+ annual revenue run-rate growing triple digits. Tepper nearly doubled his position to #1 holding, and Ackman added +1.84M shares making it his #2. Today's broad selloff is not an AWS-specific event — thesis fully intact.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) confirming AWS margin expansion and AI inference monetization
picks.entryNote: Has outperformed S&P 500 YTD; today's -2% dip in line with market, not company-specific. AWS reacceleration visible in data makes current levels attractive on a 1-3yr basis.
picks.exitTrigger: AWS growth decelerates below 20% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or operating margin compression below 8% consolidated
picks.keyRisk: FCF compression from accelerating capex ($105B+ 2026 guidance) if cloud monetization lags spend

picks.name: Oracle Corporation
picks.rationale: ORCL was added on June 3 and moved to rank #5 on June 4 as a differentiated AI cloud pick with lower direct exposure to the AVGO-specific narrative. Oracle Cloud Infrastructure is the fastest-growing major cloud at scale, winning multi-billion dollar AI training contracts from hyperscalers needing neutral third-party capacity. OCI's remaining performance obligations (RPO) backlog exceeded $130B last quarter, providing multi-year revenue visibility that is not AI-hype dependent. Today's selloff — driven by AVGO custom ASIC miss — does not touch Oracle's database/cloud SaaS franchise.
picks.catalyst: FQ4 2026 earnings (mid-June) with cloud RPO and new AI contract announcements
picks.entryNote: Pulls back with broader tech today despite no company-specific negative; trades at premium to historical multiple but justified by cloud RPO backlog growth rate. Near-term earnings are a live catalyst.
picks.exitTrigger: Cloud RPO backlog growth stalls below 20% YoY, or a major hyperscaler customer cancels infrastructure contracts
picks.keyRisk: AWS, Azure, and GCP could undercut OCI on pricing to win back AI workloads previously ceded to neutral cloud

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: Meta posted Q1 2026 EPS of $7.31 vs. $6.79 estimated (7.7% beat), with net income climbing to $26.8B from $16.6B a year earlier, and ad revenue accelerating +33% YoY — the fastest in years — powered by AI-optimized targeting. Today's additional pressure from a secondary offering is a short-term technical overhang, not a fundamental thesis break. Meta's AI agent monetization and custom silicon buildout (1GW with Broadcom) give it a durable earnings growth runway. Free cash flow generation more than justifies capex, unlike pure-infrastructure peers.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with ad ARPU acceleration and Meta AI monetization updates
picks.entryNote: Down sharply today on secondary offering news — a technical, not fundamental, event. The dip creates an improved entry vs. last week's prices for a name with 4/4 EPS beats and accelerating ad revenue growth.
picks.exitTrigger: Ad revenue growth decelerates below 15% YoY, or meaningful regulatory action restricting Meta AI agent deployment in the EU or US
picks.keyRisk: Capex-driven FCF compression if AI monetization timeline slips beyond 2027; EU AI Act compliance costs

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman is the cleanest capital-markets beneficiary in a late-cycle environment: the M&A and IPO revival cycle (Anthropic IPO filed confidentially, Quantinuum IPO, CoreWeave) directly flows through GS's advisory and underwriting businesses. Elevated rates sustain net interest income while the steep 2s10s curve (+42 bps) benefits FICC trading. The sector is confirmed outperformer with robust bank profitability per sector research. Today's selloff barely touched Financials (Dow actually edged higher +0.07% intraday) — relative strength confirmed.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) with M&A advisory revenue and FICC trading results
picks.entryNote: Outperforming on a relative basis today as Financials rotate into favor away from tech selloff. Trading at fair value vs. book with improving ROE trajectory as dealmaking cycle reopens.
picks.exitTrigger: Investment banking revenue misses and management guides down M&A pipeline, or credit provisions surge >$2B in a single quarter signaling credit deterioration
picks.keyRisk: Fed hike cycle compresses credit quality faster than anticipated, spiking loan loss provisions across the banking sector

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the premier defense/aerospace compounder with direct exposure to the two dominant macro tailwinds: Iran war-driven defense spending surge and commercial aerospace recovery. The Iran conflict was mentioned in 42% of ISM Manufacturing comments in May, directly supporting defense procurement. GE Aerospace's +87% order growth in Q1 confirms aerospace demand is structural, and RTX's Pratt & Whitney engine business rides the same commercial aviation wave with its own defense radar and missile systems adding a geopolitical premium. phaseData.macro:
Here is your senior macro strategy briefing for **Friday, June 5, 2026**:

---

## 📊 MACRO REGIME DASHBOARD — US EQUITIES | June 5, 2026

---

### THE DATA TAPE

**FED POLICY**

The Federal Reserve maintained the federal funds rate at 3.50%–3.75%
, now 
the third consecutive meeting where the committee chose to stand pat – following three consecutive cuts last year.
 The April 29 meeting was unusually contentious: 
the FOMC split along 8–4 lines, with officials expressing different reasons for their vote
 — 
the last time four members dissented was October 1992.
 On the dot plot, 
the median of the March 2026 Fed dot plot projections sees the fed funds rate falling just 25 bps in 2026 and 25 bps in 2027,
 implying a terminal rate around 3.25%. 
Notably, the "longer run" anchor has shifted upward to 3.0%, signaling a collective belief that the neutral rate is higher than previously estimated.
 
Fed officials foresee the funds rate settling down to its expected neutral level around 3.1%.
 Market-implied path: 
markets are pricing in no changes the rest of this year and well into 2027,
 with 
an ~85% chance of a quarter-point rate *hike* by year-end, up from 60% a week ago,
 as energy-driven inflation reaccelerates. The real policy rate (Fed funds midpoint 3.625% vs. core PCE ~3.3%) is barely positive — policy is only modestly restrictive relative to neutral at ~3.0–3.1%.

**YIELD CURVE**

As of June 4, 2026, the US Treasury yield curve is upward-sloping: the 2-Year yields 4.05%, the 10-Year 4.48%, and the 30-Year 4.98%.
 
The 10Y vs. 2Y spread (2s10s) is +42 bps; the 10Y vs. 3M spread (3m10y) is +76 bps.
 Crucially, 
the 10-year yield hovered around 4.48% after climbing on stronger-than-expected labor market data, reinforcing expectations of tighter Fed policy.
 
Elevated term premium and steady Treasury issuance continue to support higher long-maturity yields, consistent with upward pressure on long-end benchmarks.
 Term premium is rising — structurally driven by persistent inflation uncertainty and heavy sovereign supply.

**INFLATION — RE-ACCELERATING**

The annual inflation rate accelerated to 3.8% in April 2026, the highest since May 2023, compared to 3.3% in March — above forecasts of 3.7% — as the oil shock triggered by the war with Iran continues to push prices higher.
 
Energy costs jumped 17.9%, the steepest annual increase since September 2022, with gasoline up 28.4% and fuel oil up 54.3%.
 
Core CPI edged higher to 2.8% year-on-year, the highest since September, from 2.6% in March.
 On PCE: 
the PCE price index rose 0.4% in April (3.8% y/y), marking the third consecutive month of rapid inflation; the core PCE rose 0.2% (3.3% y/y).
 The March SEP projected 
a 30-bps increase in yearend 2026 PCE inflation (now 2.7%).
 
The softer-than-expected 0.24% m/m gain in core PCE in April buys the Fed a bit of time as it waits to see how higher oil prices will feed through to broader inflation pressures.
 Trajectory: **re-accelerating**, energy-led with secondary shelter stickiness.

**GROWTH**

Per the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow model, real GDP growth is currently tracking at 3% (quarter-over-quarter annualized basis) in Q2.
 However, 
Q1 GDP growth was revised down to 1.6% SAAR from 2.0% in the advance estimate, with the downward revision almost entirely due to a larger inventory drawdown.
 On labor: 
NFP is expected to rise ~85K in May (today's release), slowing from the 115K gain in April; the unemployment rate is forecast to hold at 4.3%.
 The three-month payroll run-rate is modest (~125–160K/month based on March's +178K and April's +115K). 
The third consecutive consensus-beating gain in nonfarm payrolls in May, if confirmed, should further reduce FOMC concern about downside labor market risks.
 On ISMs: 
Manufacturing PMI registered 54% in May, its highest reading since May 2022.
 
Among surveyed manufacturers, the Iran war was mentioned in 42% of comments and tariffs in 18%; 57% cited pricing volatility as an issue.
 Services: 
ISM Services PMI increased to 54.5 in May from 53.6 in April, the strongest gain in three months.
 
Employment contracted for a third consecutive month (47.9), while price pressures intensified to the highest since August 2022 (71.3) — diesel, gasoline, and oil again the primary culprits.


**CREDIT & LIQUIDITY**

As of early 2026, broad investment-grade OAS is roughly ~80 bps.
 HY OAS data from FRED is current but specific levels were unavailable in today's pull — directionally, 
the HY-IG spread is trending down and SPX is rising, indicative of near-term risk-on sentiment.
 DXY: 
the US Dollar Index (DXY) fell toward 99.18 before recovering to ~99.45 on Thursday, as investors digested Fed commentary ahead of today's NFP release.
 Financial conditions are loose overall — the curve is positively sloped, spreads are tight, and dollar weakness has provided an additional easing impulse. 
Treasury yields have found support from escalating Middle East tensions, which keep oil prices elevated and add to inflationary pressures.


**EARNINGS BACKDROP**

The S&P 500 climbed to another record high recently, closing at 7,580.
 
The index extended its advance for a ninth consecutive week, rising more than 5% in May.
 
Technology EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February 2026,
 driven by AI infrastructure demand. 
The forward P/E on the S&P 500 stands at approximately 25.9x (Dec 2026 basis)
 — materially above the 10-year average of ~18–19x. 
The four largest hyperscalers — Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta — collectively spent north of $400 billion in capex in 2025, nearly 70% more than in 2024.
 The spread between AI capex intensity and near-term earnings delivery is a latent valuation risk.

**GEOPOLITICAL / EVENT TAPE**

A string of resilient US economic indicators has reinforced hawkish Fed expectations, while peace negotiations between the US and Iran are reportedly nearing their final stage — but Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said no meaningful progress had been made, and Iran-backed Hezbollah rejected a US-mediated ceasefire proposal, keeping geopolitical risks elevated.
 
Policymakers face an economic climate where inflation has held well above the Fed's 2% target, as tariffs and soaring energy prices complicate policy.
 
The next FOMC meeting is June 16–17, 2026.


---

## 🔴 PARAGRAPH 1 — REGIME CALL

**LATE-CYCLE.** The regime exhibits the classic late-cycle signature across all major pillars: 
positive but compressed spreads (2s10s +42 bps, 3m10y +76 bps) consistent with a cautious growth outlook and persistent inflation pressures that keep the Fed on hold.
 Nominal growth is still positive — GDPNow tracking +3% in Q2 — but Q1 was revised down to a soft 1.6%, labor is hiring at a decelerating pace, and 
services employment has now contracted for three consecutive months.
 Meanwhile, inflation is re-accelerating (CPI 3.8%, PCE 3.8% y/y) in a supply-shock context, leaving the Fed frozen on the sideline with a real policy rate barely above zero. This is textbook late-cycle: growth still above trend but losing momentum, inflation uncomfortably sticky/rising, policy rates on hold with a hawkish drift, term premium expanding, and equity markets priced for perfection at ~26x forward earnings.

---

## 🟡 PARAGRAPH 2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**What's Working:** Energy and defense are natural beneficiaries of the Iran war premium — 
S&P 500 forward EPS revisions are still trending higher even since the start of the Iran conflict, with energy estimates getting a boost.
 
Manufacturing at a 4-year high ISM (54%) with 19 straight months of broad expansion
 lifts Industrials and Materials. 
Technology estimates have been revised sharply higher,
 supported by the AI capex super-cycle; large-cap Tech/Semis (particularly AI infrastructure names like Oracle and Micron) are carrying the index to record highs. Quality/Profitability factors screen well in late cycle — companies with pricing power and low variable cost exposure to energy. Short-duration equities (Financials with steeper curve; Energy) and real assets outperform. **What's Breaking:** Rate-sensitive sectors are under pressure: 
inflation elevated, energy prices rising
 — this squeezes Consumer Discretionary margins and Housing. 
The prices-paid index at multi-year highs signals mounting supply-chain stress and margin pressure, likely restraining near-term investment and production for mid-cap Industrials.
 
Services employment is contracting for a third consecutive month
 — a leading stress indicator for labor-intensive sectors (Retail, Healthcare Services). Long-duration growth/SMID cap are most vulnerable to any further yield backup. The 26x forward P/E leaves essentially zero margin of safety for negative earnings surprises.

---

## 🟠 PARAGRAPH 3 — WHAT TO WATCH

**1. Today's May Nonfarm Payrolls (June 5) + June CPI (June 10):** 
Investors are awaiting today's nonfarm payrolls report for further clues on labor market strength
 — consensus expects ~85K but 
the third consecutive consensus-beating print
 would further close the door on any 2026 rate cut and 
markets are already pricing an 85% chance of a *hike* by year-end.
 June 10 CPI (May data) is equally pivotal: 
the Consumer Price Index for May 2026 is scheduled to be released on June 10 at 8:30 a.m.
 A third consecutive hot print would materially reprice the entire rates and equity complex. **2. June 16–17 FOMC Meeting + New Dot Plot:** 
The next FOMC meeting is June 16–17.
 With the April vote fracturing 8–4 and 
a majority of participants flagging that policy firming could become appropriate if inflation continues to run persistently above 2%,
 this meeting carries a live risk of a hawkish pivot in language or even a surprise hike — an event that would severely stress equities at current valuations. **3. Middle East Ceasefire/Escalation Binary:** 
Trump says peace talks are nearing their final stage, but Iran's foreign minister reports no meaningful progress, and Hezbollah has rejected a US-mediated Lebanon ceasefire,
 keeping the oil shock live. A genuine ceasefire would deliver a sharp disinflationary impulse — unlocking Fed cuts, compressing yields, and rotating the tape from Energy/Defense into rate-sensitive laggards. Escalation does the opposite and forces the Fed's hand higher. This is the single highest-optionality event on the 1–6 month horizon.

## 📊 US EQUITY MACRO BRIEFING — JUNE 8, 2026

### Key Data Dashboard

**FED POLICY**

The current Fed funds target rate stands at **4.25%**.

**YIELD CURVE**

As of June 5, the Treasury curve is upward-sloping: **3-month = 3.72%**, **2-year = 4.17%**, **5-year = 4.29%**, **10-year = 4.55%**, **30-year = 5.01%**.
 
The **2s10s spread is +38 bps** and the **3m10y spread is +83 bps**.
 
These positive spreads reflect a cautious growth outlook with persistent inflation pressures, while elevated term premium and steady Treasury issuance continue to support higher long-maturity yields.
 
The broader shape — front end inverted relative to policy just months ago, belly flat, long end elevated — retains a classic late-cycle signature.


**INFLATION**

Headline CPI re-accelerated to **3.8% y/y** in April 2026 (the highest since May 2023, vs. 3.3% in March), coming in above the 3.7% forecast, as the oil shock from the US-Iran war continues to push prices higher.
 
Energy costs jumped **+17.9% y/y** — the steepest since September 2022 — driven by gasoline (+28.4%) and fuel oil (+54.3%).
 
Core CPI edged higher to **2.8% y/y**, the highest since September, with the monthly print at **+0.4%** — above February and March's 0.2% readings.
 
Headline **PCE came in at 3.77% y/y** (April, released May 28), with a 6.02% annualized 3-month pace.
 
The Cleveland Fed Nowcast projects May CPI will print **+0.46% m/m** (pushing the annual rate to ~4.18%), while core CPI looks more contained at +0.23% m/m / 2.82% y/y.
 Inflation is clearly **re-accelerating**, not decelerating — driven by a dual supply+demand shock (Iran war energy + AI capex cycle). 
Outside the energy complex, an increasingly dominant factor in the inflation surge is a basket of goods and services tied to the AI buildout — from computer equipment to software — with meaningful momentum as the capex cycle continues.


**GROWTH**

May nonfarm payrolls surged to **172,000** — roughly doubling the 85,000 consensus estimate — while unemployment held at **4.3%**.
 
Average hourly earnings rose **+0.3% m/m** (in line). March payrolls were revised up to 214,000, with combined March+April revisions totaling +93,000.
 
Services jobs did "most of the heavy lifting," led by leisure & hospitality (+70,000) and healthcare & social assistance.
 
Q2 personal consumption is tracking strong in the nowcast, but real wage growth is currently in negative territory and the savings rate is about half of what it was before the 2022 inflation spike, squeezing real income.
 
The composite ISM Prices Paid index eased slightly to 153.4 in May from 155.3 in April, but remains highly elevated.


**CREDIT & LIQUIDITY**

Broad investment-grade (IG) OAS is roughly **~80 bps** as of early 2026.
 HY spreads have widened from recent tights on rising rate-hike fears but have not yet signaled a disorderly credit event. 
The Bloomberg Commodity Index has risen **+40.5% y/y** and +19.8% annualized over 3 months, reflecting sustained energy-price pressures tied to geopolitical uncertainties and supply constraints.
 
Yields rose sharply and stocks slid Friday as rate-hike odds climbed on the hot payrolls print.


**EARNINGS & VALUATION**

Goldman Sachs raised its S&P 500 year-end 2026 target to **8,000** (from 7,600), projecting 6% return, with **2026 EPS forecast of $340** (representing ~24% annual growth).
 
The trailing P/E on the S&P 500 stands at **25.3x** as of June 8.
 
Goldman expects the forward multiple to remain flat at roughly **21x**, as modest yield declines are offset by slowing growth, geopolitical uncertainty, and investor skepticism about AI-profit durability.
 
The historical median forward P/E is **18.2x**, placing current valuations well above long-run norms.
 
Q1 2026 earnings growth is tracking at nearly **29% y/y** per FactSet.


**GEOPOLITICAL TAPE**

Affordability concerns are unlikely to fade if the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed throughout the summer, mostly because of the likely persistence of higher energy prices.
 
Markets are pricing an **82% probability of a Bank of Japan rate hike** at its June 16 meeting — a material cross-asset risk if confirmed.


---

### ¶1 — REGIME CALL

**Late-Cycle / Stagflationary Overhang.** The US economy is firmly in a **late-cycle, stagflationary regime**: 
the curve has moved from its 2022–2024 inversion toward a narrow positive slope, but the 3m10y has flipped negative again recently — a classic late-cycle signature —
 while 
inflation continues to outstrip nominal wage gains, squeezing real incomes and underscoring the stickiness of service and energy costs.
 The Fed is effectively trapped: 
AI demand shock and Iran war supply shock are occurring simultaneously, making the Fed's path increasingly difficult to navigate.


---

### ¶2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** 
Semiconductor companies are the primary direct beneficiaries of the AI capex cycle, with tech hardware, industrials, and utilities companies also receiving a large earnings boost from the AI buildout.
 
The S&P 500 has been on pace for 10 consecutive weeks of gains, with 8 of 11 sectors rising Thursday — the kind of breadth that reinforces faith in the rally — with financials and healthcare attracting buyers on rotation out of tech.
 Energy and commodity-linked equities are working structurally given the commodity supercycle impulse. **Breaking:** 
Real inflation-adjusted wage growth is in negative territory, and today's consumer — especially at the lower end of the K-shaped economy — is in a weaker spot.
 Rate-sensitive sectors (REITs, small-cap growth, consumer discretionary ex-luxury) face headwinds as 
yields rose sharply on rate-hike odds climbing post-payrolls.
 
Only about 56% of S&P 500 stocks trade above their 50-day moving average despite the index trading near record highs
 — a warning sign of extreme top-heaviness and fragile breadth beneath the surface.

---

### ¶3 — WHAT TO WATCH

1. **May CPI (June 10, 8:30 AM ET):** 
This is the single most market-moving release of the week, with the Cleveland Fed Nowcast flagging upside risk to a 4.18% y/y headline print.
 A print above 4.2% would likely push rate-hike odds above 65% and re-price the front end sharply higher.

2. **Bank of Japan Rate Decision (June 16):** 
Japan's PPI has already accelerated to 4.9% y/y in April and is expected to push above 5.0% in May, with markets pricing 82% odds of a BOJ hike — a hot print would tip those odds close to certainty.
 A BOJ hike would trigger yen carry-trade unwinding, a potential cross-asset shock for US equities and Treasuries.

3. **Strait of Hormuz / Iran War Escalation:** 
Inflation has jumped to the top of concern, chiefly because of the rapid increase in energy's contribution to both CPI and PCE — a driver that was entirely absent from pre-2026 forecasts.
 Any further escalation that tightens oil supply materially would force the Fed's hand and compress equity multiples in a classic stagflationary derating.

## 3. 🌪️ MACRO TAIL RISKS (12-Month Horizon)

---

### ⚡ TAIL RISK #1 — **Stagflation Lock-In / Fed Policy Error**
**Probability Tier: ELEVATED (30–35%)**


If the labor market continues to weaken and inflation remains above the Fed's 2% target as tariffs trickle down to consumers, the Fed's job could become more complicated. The big concern is stagflation — a rare condition where inflation and unemployment both run high.


**Trigger mechanism:** 
An increase in inflation to 3.5% or above would create the conditions for a reversal in monetary policy, with the Fed quickly returning to a policy rate of 4% or above and the unemployment rate rising to 5% or higher.


**Current state:** 
The Fed's federal funds rate sits at 3.50%–3.75%, marking the second pause in 2026 following three consecutive cuts to close out 2025.
 
BofA Global Research now believes cuts may not come until mid or late 2027.


**Factors/sectors most hurt:**
- **Long-duration growth stocks** (PLTR, SNOW, ARM, CRWD) — violent multiple compression as discount rates re-price higher
- **Consumer Discretionary** (TSLA, BKNG, TGT, NKE) — demand destruction at lower income strata
- **Real Estate** (PLD, EQIX, AMT) — cap rate re-expansion crushes valuations
- **TLT** — worst of both worlds: inflation prevents the safety bid while yields rise

---

### ⚡ TAIL RISK #2 — **U.S. Fiscal / Sovereign Debt Spiral**
**Probability Tier: LOW-MEDIUM (15–20%)**


America's public finances are precarious — the deficit is expected to remain at 6–7% of GDP for some time. There's a risk investors begin to baulk at the volume of debt issuance, potentially combined with a perception of too-loose monetary policy and hints of inflation.



The federal deficit is expected to remain above 6% of GDP through 2030, exceeding the Congressional Budget Office's baseline expectation.


**Trigger mechanism:** A failed Treasury auction, a sovereign downgrade (Moody's removed the U.S. AAA in May 2025), or a political standoff over the debt ceiling causes a disorderly spike in 10-year yields above 5.5%.
